divergentCases.prompt: A coat is reduced by 30%, then a further 15% off the already-reduced price. After that, 8% VAT is added on the discounted price. The customer pays exactly €92.43. What was the ORIGINAL price in euros, to two decimals?

claude-sonnet-4-20250514.sampleFinalText:
I need to work backwards from the final price to find the original price.

Let me call the original price P.

Step 1: First reduction of 30%
After 30% reduction, price = P × (1 - 0.30) = P × 0.70

Step 2: Second reduction of 15% off the already-reduced price
After second reduction, price = (P × 0.70) × (1 - 0.15) = P × 0.70 × 0.85 = P × 0.595

claude-sonnet-4-6.sampleFinalText:
I need to find the original price given the sequence of operations.

## Setting Up the Equation

Let the original price = P

**Step 1:** 30% reduction → multiply by 0.70
**Step 2:** Further 15% off → multiply by 0.85
**Step 3:** 8% VAT added → multiply by 1.08

Final price = P × 0.70 × 0.85 × 1.08 = 92.43

Step 1: Find the side length of the cube.
If the cube has volume 343 cm³, then:
side³ = 343
side = ∛343 = 7 cm

Step 2: Find the radius of the inscribed sphere.
When a sphere is inscribed in a cube (touching all six faces), the diameter of the sphere

claude-sonnet-4-5-20250929.sampleFinalText:
I need to find the surface area of a sphere inscribed in a cube with volume 343 cubic centimeters.

**Step 1: Find the side length of the cube.**

If the cube has volume V = 343 cm³, and volume = s³ where s is the side length:
- s³ = 343
- s = ∛343 = 7 cm

**Step 2: Find the radius of the inscribed sphere.**

When a sphere is inscribed in a cube (t

claude-sonnet-4-6.sampleFinalText:
The cube has volume 343 cm³, so the side length is ∛343 = 7 cm.

The inscribed sphere touches all six faces, so its diameter equals the side length of the cube.
Diameter = 7 cm, so radius = 3.5 cm.

Surface area of sphere = 4πr² = 4 × 3.14159 × (3.5)²
= 4 × 3.14159 × 12.25
= 4 × 38.484827...
= 153.93930...

Rounded to two decimals: 153.94

<answer>

impactScenarios.title: 1. The silent regression — same code, model bumped, fewer bookings complete
impactScenarios.finding: On tool use, Sonnet 4 → 4.5 dropped correctness 0.91 → 0.87 (Δ −4.0 percentage points). Sonnet 4.6 then recovered to 0.92. The regression is real, not noise: n=30 per cell, stdev ≈ 0.27.
impactScenarios.concreteExample: Anna books a Stockholm weekend trip via your travel-planning agent on Sonnet 4. The agent reliably searches flights, suggests an Old Town hotel and warns about the weather — 91% of bookings completed end-to-end. Tuesday, ops rolls a "minor model bump to Sonnet 4.5" for the better long-context support. Wednesday, Anna receives flight options but no hotel — the agent stopped after one tool call. Across 10 000 weekly bookings, that is ~400 extra half-completed trips.
impactScenarios.productionConsequence: 4 percentage points down means roughly 1 in 25 bookings now needs human follow-up. No alert fires because the API returned 200 OK; the agent just stopped calling tools earlier. Discovered three weeks in when finance sees hotel-affiliate revenue down 8% and support tickets up 30%. The "minor bump" cost a sprint of incident response.
impactScenarios.mitigation: Run the 10 tool-use cases × 3 replicates against every candidate model before promotion. A ≥ 2 pp drop on the headline metric blocks the rollout in CI. This sweep cost roughly $0.65 per Sonnet model — cheaper than one undetected regression week.

impactScenarios.title: 4. Tier mismatch — picking too cheap a tier costs human handle time
impactScenarios.finding: On the classification + reply task, mean correctness: Haiku 0.79 → Sonnet 4.6 0.87 → Opus 4.7 0.91. At 5,000 tickets/day that is 1067 misrouted on Haiku vs 643 on Sonnet 4.6 vs 428 on Opus 4.7.
impactScenarios.concreteExample: Your support ops run a routing agent: classify ticket → reply or escalate. The team chose Haiku 4.5 because it was 16× cheaper than Opus. At 5,000 tickets/day, Haiku misroutes 1067 of them (21.3%). Each misroute costs ~6 minutes of human handle time when caught downstream: 107 extra hours of L1 work per day. At €30/hour, ≈€3,201/day.
impactScenarios.productionConsequence: Annualised, choosing Haiku over Sonnet 4.6 on this task costs ≈ €318,000 of avoidable handle time per year (working-days basis). The Haiku savings on API cost are an order of magnitude smaller. Without the eval comparison, the buyer never sees this trade.
impactScenarios.mitigation: Cost/accuracy scatter (section 7) makes this visible at a glance: Sonnet 4.6 sits on the Pareto frontier for this task. Use Haiku for tasks where the eval shows it is on the frontier (here, extraction is one such); use Sonnet 4.6 for classification.

impactScenarios.title: 5. Variance ≠ determinism — you cannot buy determinism from an LLM
impactScenarios.finding: On classification, Haiku 4.5 shows stdev 0.202 across replicates while Opus 4.7 shows stdev 0.139 — roughly an 1× difference. Higher tiers are quieter, but neither is zero. Same prompt, same temperature 0, the model can still produce a different answer on the next call.
impactScenarios.concreteExample: Quarterly compliance audit. The auditor samples 30 support replies and asks the team to re-generate them. On Haiku, three of the 30 come back with a different intent label — the same input produced "refund" twice and "complaint" once across the replicates. The team's first instinct is "upgrade to Opus, it is 8× less noisy." But Opus's stdev of 0.139 is still strictly greater than zero — on a long enough tail of inputs, it will also disagree with itself. The auditor's bar is not "low variance"; it is "identical input ⇒ identical output, always." No LLM tier clears that bar.
impactScenarios.productionConsequence: Higher-tier models reduce stochastic variance but cannot eliminate it. If your compliance regime, regulator, or contract demands true determinism, no Claude tier — Opus included — meets that bar. Paying the Opus premium to "buy determinism" is solving the wrong problem at the wrong layer; the auditor will find the residual variance eventually, and the documentation that called Opus "deterministic" becomes the liability.
impactScenarios.mitigation: Don't try to buy determinism from an LLM — extract it. (1) If "intent → routing rule" must be deterministic for compliance, move that decision to a classical classifier or a rules table; reserve the LLM for the parts that genuinely need language understanding, such as drafting the reply once routing is already decided. (2) If "low behavioural variance" is what you actually need, quantify the bar (e.g. ≥98% identical-output rate on a regression set) and pick the cheapest tier that clears it — the variance box plot in section 8 is the procurement evidence. (3) Compose: tiered LLM for generation + deterministic logic for compliance-critical branching. The Opus cost premium is justified where the lower variance translates to measurable downstream value — not as a substitute for determinism that the LLM cannot provide.

impactScenarios.title: 6. Pareto economics — naive 'always Opus' leaves real money on the table
impactScenarios.finding: Across this sweep, mean cost per call: Haiku $0.0019, Sonnet 4.6 $0.0066, Opus 4.7 $0.0341 — Opus is 5.2× Sonnet 4.6 for an overall +2.3 pp on correctness.
impactScenarios.concreteExample: Stakeholder defaults to Opus for everything because "the best model". At 50,000 interactions/month: $1705/month, blended correctness 0.938. Routed alternative: Sonnet 4.6 for the 80% of interactions where the eval shows it matches Opus (extraction, reasoning, easy classification) + Opus for the 20% of hard cases. Routed monthly cost: $605. Annual saving: ≈$13193 with negligible quality drop.
impactScenarios.productionConsequence: Without the cost/accuracy chart, the procurement decision is "pick the headline number". With it, you can defend Sonnet-4.6-by-default to the CFO and Opus-when-flagged to engineering. Same SLO, lower TCO, documented trade-off.
impactScenarios.mitigation: The cost/accuracy scatter (section 7) and tier comparison (section 5) are the artefacts to put in front of finance. Re-run the eval every quarter — pricing and quality both move.
